RLLM Serving

Reasoning Language Model Inference Serving Unveiled:
An Empirical Study

Qi Li1,2*, Junpan Wu4*, Xiang Liu2*, Yuxin Wang3, Zeyu Li2, Yuhan Chen2, Shaohuai Shi5,
Zhenheng Tang6† Xiaowen Chu2†
1Tsinghua University, 2The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guang Zhou),
3HKBU, 4University of Wisconsin-Madison, 5Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen,
6The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
* Equal contribution † Corresponding author
ICLR 2026
OpenReview arXiv Code
Overview Key Findings Pilot Study Optimization Real-world Workload Citation

Abstract

The reasoning large language model (RLLM) has been proven competitive in solving complex reasoning tasks such as mathematics, coding, compared to general LLM. However, the serving performance and behavior of RLLM remains unexplored, which may undermine the deployment and utilization of RLLM in real-world scenario. To close this gap, in this paper, we conduct a comprehensive study of RLLM service. We first perform a pilot study on comparing the serving performance between RLLM and traditional LLM and reveal that there are several distinct differences regarding serving behavior: (1) significant memory usage and fluctuations; (2) straggler requests; (3) adaptive running time; (4) domain preference. Then we further investigate whether existing inference optimization techniques are valid for RLLM. Our main takeaways are that model quantization methods and speculative decoding can improve service system efficiency with a small compromise to RLLM accuracy, while prefix caching, KV cache quantization may even degrade accuracy or serving performance for small RLLM. Lastly, we conductevaluation under real world workload modeled by Gamma distribution to verify our findings. Empirical results for real world workload evaluation across different dataset are aligned with our main findings regarding RLLM serving. We hope our work can provide the research community and industry with insights to advance RLLM inference serving.

Paper at a Glance

Why this paper matters

This work studies a gap that most LLM serving systems were not designed for: long, adaptive reasoning traces. The paper proposes ASU (Accuracy, Service-end, User-end) and the ASU-Perf benchmark suite to evaluate RLLM serving under both controlled batch settings and bursty real-world workloads.

A

Accuracy

Serving optimizations are judged against benchmark accuracy, so efficiency gains never hide quality regressions on GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME-2024, and GPQA.

S

Service-end

The provider view focuses on throughput, especially tokens per second (TPS), to capture how effectively the system uses GPU memory and compute.

U

User-end

The user view emphasizes time to first visible token (TTFVT) and end-to-end latency, reflecting how quickly a reasoning model feels responsive in practice.

Benchmarking Setup

LLM vs. RLLM across multiple scales

The study compares Qwen-2.5 and DeepSeek-R1-Distill counterparts at 7B, 14B, 32B, and 70B, using both vLLM and SGLang for inference serving.

Pilot Study

Serving behavior is fundamentally different

Batch inference logs show that reasoning models create much larger KV cache pressure, stronger straggler effects, and runtimes that adapt to task difficulty.

Optimization Study

Do classic LLM tricks still work?

The paper tests model weight quantization, KV cache quantization, prefix caching, and speculative decoding to see which methods still help when serving RLLMs.

Production Perspective

Validated under Gamma-distributed workloads

Beyond synchronized batches, the authors replay bursty real-world request streams and observe the same pressure patterns: higher KV usage, more running requests, and longer queueing risk for RLLMs.

Key Findings

The paper highlights four recurring behaviors that separate reasoning LLM serving from conventional LLM serving.

Finding 1

Significant Memory Usage and Fluctuations

Because long chains of thought remain alive until completion, RLLM KV cache usage can swing sharply instead of staying flat. The paper reports extreme cases ranging from roughly 3% to 70% KV usage, while matched LLM runs stay far lower.

Finding 2

Straggler Requests

Hard problems can keep a small tail of requests running long after easy requests finish. Those stragglers extend batch completion time, reduce utilization, and create visible long-tail latency.

Finding 3

Adaptive Running Time

RLLM runtime tracks task difficulty much more strongly than input length alone. On harder benchmarks, the same model with the same token budget can spend far longer decoding than a conventional LLM.

Finding 4

Domain Preference on Math

Reasoning models open a clear gap on mathematical reasoning tasks while staying much closer to conventional LLMs on knowledge-centric datasets such as GPQA.

Pilot Investigations

Serving Optimization Results

The paper evaluates several widely used LLM serving optimizations and checks whether they still preserve RLLM accuracy while improving system efficiency.

Real-world Workload Evaluation

From synchronized batches to bursty request streams

To validate the batch-study findings, the paper replays workload bursts modeled by a Gamma distribution. The same core behaviors persist under these more realistic traces: reasoning models keep more work in flight, push KV cache much harder, and leave less slack for new requests to enter the engine.

Takeaways for Future RLLM Serving Systems

In the paper's final discussion, the authors outline several system directions motivated by the empirical results above.

Takeaway 1

Finer-grained memory management

Long chains of thought make memory pressure both higher and more volatile, suggesting better KV lifetime control, selective swapping/offloading, and more adaptive cache management.

Takeaway 2

Difficulty-aware scheduling

Because a few hard requests can dominate completion time, the paper argues for runtime-adaptive scheduling that can prioritize, reorder, or otherwise isolate stragglers.

Takeaway 3

Decode-heavy resource allocation

RLLMs are more decode-heavy than standard LLMs, so resource allocation should increasingly favor the decode stage instead of treating prefill and decode as balanced workloads.

Takeaway 4

Hardware-software co-design for KV access

Longer outputs intensify bandwidth and KV-access demand, creating opportunities for memory-hierarchy-aware runtimes, KV-optimized accelerators, and lower-latency decode paths.
